Expose a Local Server to the Internet With ngrok (Windows + macOS Setup)

ngrok is a handy tool for spinning up a public URL that forwards to a local port — about 5 minutes of setup and outside requests can reach your localhost. The first-run setup has a few quirks though, so this post walks through Windows + macOS setup.
Click "Sign up for free" or the "Sign up" button in the top right:
If you have a GitHub account, I'd recommend signing in with GitHub — saves the email step and you don't have to remember a separate password.
After registering, you land on the ngrok dashboard which shows you OS-specific install instructions (I'm on macOS):
Download and unzip to get the ngrok binary:
Below — OS-specific setup for both macOS and Windows.
On macOS, double-clicking ngrok hits a Gatekeeper warning:
This is macOS's default security behavior. Open System Settings → Privacy & Security:
Scroll to the Security section and click "Open Anyway":
Now the original warning lets you proceed:
But after opening, the terminal closes with "Process completed" and you can't actually use it:
in a regular terminal gives you
. That's because ngrok isn't on your PATH yet.
Open Finder and drag the ngrok binary into
. To navigate there in Finder, click "Go" → "Go to Folder", type
, hit Go. Drag the ngrok binary into that folder.
Windows doesn't hit the Gatekeeper issue, but adding to PATH is more involved. Put the ngrok binary into
(or wherever you put it). Click OK:
so the Path entry points correctly.
Back in the ngrok dashboard, find the "Connect your account" section — it gives you a one-line command. Paste it into your terminal:
Authtoken saved to configuration file: /xxx Support/ngrok/ngrok.yml
(You only have to do this once per machine.)
The only command you need to remember —
where xxxx is the local port you want to expose.
Example — I have an Express project running on port 3000 via
Most of the output is reference info. The key field is
— that's the public URL ngrok created. Copy it and share with whoever you want to reach your local app.
every restart of ngrok generates a new Forwarding URL
, unless you have a paid plan with reserved domains.

ngrok is great for ad-hoc situations — sharing a local prototype with a client or coworker, testing LINE Bot webhooks, validating an OAuth callback. Way faster than standing up a full server just for testing.

ngrok is a handy way to expose a local dev server to the internet — roughly 5 minutes of setup gets you a public HTTPS URL that forwards to your localhost. The first-run setup has a few quirks (especially on macOS — Gatekeeper blocks the binary; PATH needs configuring), so this post walks through Windows + macOS setup end to end. Useful for sharing a local prototype, testing webhooks, or wiring up a Line Bot without deploying.
